agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Bird's domain rule mandates sub-30-second freshness for courier location data shown to customers. The proposed PostgreSQL-only architecture creates a domain-architecture conflict: while PostgreSQL can store location history reliably, it is not optimized for the high-frequency write/read pattern required by real-time tracking with strict staleness guarantees. The recommended architecture introduces an in-memory store (Redis) as the hot-path for current location reads, with PostgreSQL serving as the durable store for location history. A staleness gate component enforces Bird's 30-second rule at the read boundary, ensuring no stale data ever reaches customers. This conflict is escalated explicitly.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"CourierLocationIngress","responsibility":"Receives location updates from courier devices via WebSocket or HTTP. Validates payload (lat, lng, timestamp, courier_id). Writes to both the real-time store and the durable store.","boundaries":"Owns the ingest protocol and validation. Does not interpret location data beyond structural validation."},{"name":"RealTimeLocationStore (Redis)","responsibility":"Holds the latest location per courier as a key-value pair with TTL. Each key auto-expires after 30 seconds, enforcing the staleness invariant at the storage level. This is the ONLY read source for customer-facing location queries.","boundaries":"Ephemeral, single-purpose. No history, no aggregation. Bounded to current-state reads only."},{"name":"LocationHistoryStore (PostgreSQL)","responsibility":"Durable append-only store for all location updates. Used for analytics, route reconstruction, dispute resolution, and audit trails. NEVER used for customer-facing real-time reads.","boundaries":"Write-heavy, read-for-analytics. Partitioned by time. Not on the hot read path for customers."},{"name":"StalenessGate","responsibility":"A thin enforcement layer on the read path. Before returning any location to a customer, it checks (current_time - location.timestamp) < 30s. If stale, returns a 'location unavailable' response rather than stale data. Defense-in-depth on top of Redis TTL.","boundaries":"Sits between the API layer and RealTimeLocationStore. Stateless. Pure validation."},{"name":"CustomerTrackingAPI","responsibility":"Serves customer-facing location requests. Supports polling (GET) and real-time push (WebSocket/SSE). Reads exclusively from RealTimeLocationStore through StalenessGate.","boundaries":"Customer-facing boundary. No direct database access. No writes."},{"name":"CourierTrackingAPI","responsibility":"Serves courier-side operations: accepting location pushes, returning delivery assignments. Feeds into CourierLocationIngress.","boundaries":"Courier-facing boundary. Write path only for location data."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"CourierDevice","to":"CourierLocationIngress","contract":"POST /locations or WebSocket frame: {courier_id, lat, lng, timestamp, accuracy_m}","coupling_type":"loose (protocol-based)"},{"from":"CourierLocationIngress","to":"RealTimeLocationStore","contract":"SET courier:{id} -> {lat, lng, timestamp} with TTL=30s","coupling_type":"tight (runtime dependency, but replaceable)"},{"from":"CourierLocationIngress","to":"LocationHistoryStore","contract":"INSERT into courier_locations(courier_id, lat, lng, timestamp, accuracy_m) — async via write buffer","coupling_type":"loose (async, can lag without affecting real-time path)"},{"from":"CustomerTrackingAPI","to":"StalenessGate","contract":"getLocation(courier_id) -> Location | LocationUnavailable","coupling_type":"tight (synchronous call)"},{"from":"StalenessGate","to":"RealTimeLocationStore","contract":"GET courier:{id} -> {lat, lng, timestamp} | null","coupling_type":"tight (runtime dependency)"}],"data_flow":"1. Courier device sends location update → CourierLocationIngress validates → writes to Redis (SET with 30s TTL) AND enqueues async write to PostgreSQL. 2. Customer requests courier location → CustomerTrackingAPI → StalenessGate reads from Redis → if key exists and timestamp < 30s old, return location; if key expired or timestamp stale, return 'location unavailable'. 3. PostgreSQL receives batched writes for durable history. No customer-facing read path touches PostgreSQL for current location.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)","rationale":"Write path (ingress → Redis + PostgreSQL) is separated from read path (Redis only for real-time). Different stores optimized for different access patterns."},{"pattern":"TTL-based staleness enforcement","rationale":"Redis key expiry at 30s structurally enforces Bird's domain rule. Stale data literally ceases to exist. This makes the invariant impossible to violate at the storage layer."},{"pattern":"Defense-in-depth staleness validation","rationale":"StalenessGate provides a second timestamp check even if Redis TTL has minor clock drift. Belt and suspenders for a critical domain rule."},{"pattern":"Async write-behind to durable store","rationale":"PostgreSQL writes are buffered/async so the hot path (ingress → Redis) is not bottlenecked by disk I/O. Acceptable because history is not on the real-time critical path."},{"pattern":"Event-driven push (WebSocket/SSE)","rationale":"Polling every <30s from thousands of customers is wasteful. Push-based delivery to customers reduces load and improves perceived real-time quality."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"CourierTracking","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierLocation — value object representing a point-in-time location (courier_id, lat, lng, timestamp, accuracy). Not an entity because we care about the latest value, not identity over time.","CourierPresence — tracks whether a courier is 'live' (has a non-stale location) or 'offline'. Derived from location freshness."]},{"name":"DeliveryFulfillment","classification":"core","aggregates":["Delivery — entity with lifecycle. Consumes courier location as a read-only input. Does not own location data."]},{"name":"LocationAnalytics","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CourierRoute — reconstructed from historical location data in PostgreSQL. Used for post-hoc analysis, not real-time."]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"CourierTracking","downstream":"DeliveryFulfillment","relationship":"Published Language — CourierTracking publishes location events; DeliveryFulfillment subscribes. ACL in DeliveryFulfillment translates raw location into delivery-relevant proximity."},{"upstream":"CourierTracking","downstream":"LocationAnalytics","relationship":"Conformist — Analytics consumes the raw location history model as-is from PostgreSQL."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Introduce Redis as real-time location store instead of PostgreSQL-only","gains":"Sub-millisecond reads, natural TTL enforcement of 30s staleness rule, high write throughput for location updates, structural impossibility of serving stale data","sacrifices":"Operational complexity of running Redis alongside PostgreSQL, potential data loss of in-flight locations if Redis crashes (mitigated by async PostgreSQL writes), additional infrastructure cost","alternatives_considered":["PostgreSQL-only with application-level staleness checks — rejected because high-frequency writes create WAL pressure, vacuum overhead, and read latency under load; staleness check is purely application logic with no structural guarantee","PostgreSQL with LISTEN/NOTIFY for push — rejected because NOTIFY is not designed for high-throughput fan-out to many customers; breaks down at scale"]},{"decision":"Async write-behind to PostgreSQL rather than synchronous dual-write","gains":"Hot path latency is Redis-only (~1ms). PostgreSQL write failures don't block real-time tracking.","sacrifices":"Small window where a location update exists in Redis but not yet in PostgreSQL. If both Redis and the async buffer fail simultaneously, that update is lost from history.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous dual-write — rejected because it doubles hot-path latency and couples availability to the slower store","Write to PostgreSQL first, replicate to Redis — rejected because it puts PostgreSQL on the hot path and adds latency to the freshness-critical write"]},{"decision":"Defense-in-depth StalenessGate on read path","gains":"Protects against Redis TTL clock drift, misconfiguration, or future refactors that might bypass TTL. Makes the 30s rule explicit in code, not just infrastructure config.","sacrifices":"Minor additional latency on reads (one timestamp comparison — negligible). Slightly more code to maintain.","alternatives_considered":["Rely solely on Redis TTL — rejected because infrastructure config alone is too implicit for a critical domain invariant; a config change could silently violate Bird's rule"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Real-time store technology","evolution_path":"Redis can be swapped for any TTL-capable KV store (Memcached, DragonflyDB, etc.) or even a custom in-memory service. The interface is a simple GET/SET with TTL."},{"area":"Push delivery mechanism","evolution_path":"Start with SSE for simplicity, evolve to WebSocket if bidirectional communication is needed. The StalenessGate and store are agnostic to delivery mechanism."},{"area":"Location update frequency","evolution_path":"Currently designed for ~5-10s update intervals. If business wants sub-second, the architecture holds — Redis and WebSocket can handle it. PostgreSQL write-behind may need batching adjustments."},{"area":"Staleness threshold","evolution_path":"The 30-second threshold is parameterized in both Redis TTL config and StalenessGate. Changing it is a config update, not a code change."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Customer reads NEVER touch PostgreSQL for current location","reason":"This is a hard architectural boundary. Violating it couples real-time performance to OLTP database health and makes the staleness guarantee harder to enforce. This must be treated as an invariant of the system."},{"area":"30-second staleness rule enforced at storage AND application layer","reason":"Bird's domain rule is non-negotiable. Both TTL and StalenessGate must enforce it. Removing either layer requires explicit domain authority approval."},{"area":"Location data is append-only in PostgreSQL","reason":"Historical location data must never be mutated. It serves as audit trail and analytics source. UPDATE/DELETE operations on location history are forbidden."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Redis unavailability causes total loss of real-time tracking for customers","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Run Redis in HA mode (Sentinel or Cluster). If Redis is down, CustomerTrackingAPI returns 'location temporarily unavailable' — never falls back to PostgreSQL (which would bypass staleness guarantees and set a bad precedent)."},{"risk":"Clock skew between courier devices and server causes premature or delayed staleness","severity":"high","mitigation":"Server timestamps all locations at ingress time (server_received_at), not relying on device clocks. StalenessGate uses server_received_at for freshness checks."},{"risk":"High-frequency location writes overwhelm PostgreSQL async write buffer","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Batch writes to PostgreSQL (e.g., every 5 seconds per courier). Implement backpressure: if buffer is full, drop oldest un-persisted updates (acceptable for analytics, not for real-time)."},{"risk":"Customers see 'location unavailable' too frequently if courier update intervals are close to 30s","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Courier app should target 10-15s update intervals, providing 2x safety margin. Monitor staleness-gate rejection rate as an operational metric."},{"risk":"Async write-behind loses location history on simultaneous Redis + buffer failure","severity":"low","mitigation":"Acceptable for analytics use case. If auditability is legally required, consider synchronous write to a lightweight WAL or message queue (Kafka) before async fan-out."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Implement CourierLocationIngress writing to Redis with 30s TTL. Implement StalenessGate and CustomerTrackingAPI reading from Redis. Ship this for real-time tracking. Phase 2: Add async write-behind to PostgreSQL for location history. Phase 3: Add WebSocket/SSE push to customers. This ordering delivers the core domain rule (freshness) first, then durability, then UX polish.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/courier-tracking/ingress/location-ingress.service.ts — validates and routes incoming location updates","src/courier-tracking/store/realtime-location.repository.ts — Redis GET/SET with TTL","src/courier-tracking/store/location-history.repository.ts — PostgreSQL append-only writes","src/courier-tracking/api/customer-tracking.controller.ts — customer-facing read endpoint","src/courier-tracking/domain/staleness-gate.ts — timestamp validation, 30s threshold (parameterized)","src/courier-tracking/domain/courier-location.vo.ts — value object: courier_id, lat, lng, timestamp, accuracy","src/courier-tracking/config/tracking.config.ts — staleness threshold, Redis TTL, update interval targets","migrations/xxx_create_courier_locations.sql — PostgreSQL table: courier_id, lat, lng, server_received_at, device_timestamp, accuracy_m, partitioned by time"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use value objects for location data (immutable, no identity). StalenessGate is a pure domain service — no I/O, just timestamp comparison. Repositories abstract store technology. Config is externalized. All timestamps are server-side (UTC).","pitfalls_to_avoid":["NEVER allow a fallback from Redis to PostgreSQL for customer-facing reads — this silently violates the staleness rule under pressure, exactly when it matters most","Do not use device-reported timestamps for staleness — clocks drift, devices lie","Do not store 'current location' in PostgreSQL and poll it — this defeats the entire real-time architecture","Do not skip StalenessGate because Redis TTL seems sufficient — TTL is infrastructure, StalenessGate is domain logic; both are required","Do not fan out NOTIFY from PostgreSQL to customers — it does not scale and couples read path to the write-optimized store"]},"escalations":[{"type":"domain_architecture_conflict","description":"Bird's domain rule requires sub-30-second freshness for displayed courier locations. The engineering team's proposal to store locations directly in PostgreSQL and read from it for customer display creates a conflict: PostgreSQL's write-ahead log, vacuum overhead, and row-level locking under high-frequency writes (thousands of couriers × updates every 10s) will introduce variable latency on reads. More critically, enforcing the 30-second staleness rule purely in application code against PostgreSQL has no structural guarantee — a slow query, a missing WHERE clause, or an ORM misconfiguration could silently serve stale data. The domain rule demands a storage mechanism where staleness is structurally impossible to violate (TTL-based expiry), not merely checked by application logic. PostgreSQL is the right store for durable history but the wrong store for the real-time hot path.","options":["Option A: PostgreSQL-only with application-level staleness WHERE clause. Simple infrastructure, but staleness enforcement is purely logical — one bug and stale data reaches customers. Under load, write contention degrades read latency unpredictably.","Option B: Redis (TTL-enforced) for real-time reads + PostgreSQL for durable history. Two stores to operate, but staleness is structurally guaranteed by key expiry. Hot path is sub-millisecond. Clear separation of real-time vs analytical concerns.","Option C: PostgreSQL with unlogged table for current location + application staleness check. Compromise — faster writes (no WAL), but still no structural staleness guarantee and data loss on crash."],"recommendation":"Option B. The 30-second staleness rule is a hard domain invariant from Bird. It deserves structural enforcement, not just application-level checking. The operational cost of Redis is well understood and justified by the criticality of the rule. This is not premature optimization — it is matching the architecture to the domain constraint."}],"confidence":{"level":52,"high_confidence_areas":["The need for a TTL-based store to structurally enforce the 30-second rule","CQRS separation between real-time reads and durable history","StalenessGate as defense-in-depth on the read path","PostgreSQL is wrong for the customer-facing real-time read path"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact courier fleet size and update frequency — affects whether Redis single-node suffices or cluster is needed","Whether the team has Redis operational experience — affects feasibility of Option B","Legal/compliance requirements for location data durability — could change the async write-behind trade-off","Whether WebSocket infrastructure exists or needs to be built from scratch"],"assumptions":["Courier fleet is in the hundreds-to-thousands range","Location updates arrive every 10-15 seconds per courier","The team can operate Redis in production (or use a managed service)","Location history in PostgreSQL can tolerate seconds of lag behind real-time","No regulatory requirement for synchronous durable writes of every location update"]}}
